options: ISCM — Information Security Continuous Monitoring as an ongoing cycle
audit-cm01.explanation: 800-137 defines continuous monitoring as a repeating six-step cycle.

audit-cm01.text: Which tier owns governance and strategy in ISCM?
audit-cm01.explanation: Org-tier governance sets the monitoring strategy; the system tier executes.

audit-cm01.text: Should monitoring frequency be the same for all systems?
options: No — it scales with system criticality (HIGH-impact ≈ hourly)
audit-cm01.explanation: Higher-impact systems are monitored far more frequently.

audit-cm01.text: Why did the OPM breach go undetected for over a year?
options: Absence of a functioning ISCM program with anomaly detection
audit-cm01.explanation: No working continuous-monitoring program meant the intrusion went unseen.

audit-cm01.text: The core idea of ISCM is…
options: Ongoing, frequency-tiered monitoring instead of point-in-time checks
audit-cm01.explanation: Continuous monitoring replaces snapshots with an ongoing cycle.

audit-cm01.text: The ISCM cycle ends by reviewing/updating the strategy so that…
options: The program continuously improves rather than going stale
audit-cm01.explanation: The final review step feeds back into the next cycle.

options: Scarce monitoring effort concentrates on the highest-impact systems
Low systems get the most attention
audit-cm01.explanation: Risk-based frequency focuses resources where impact is greatest.

audit-cm01.text: A functioning ISCM program primarily reduces…
options: The time an intrusion goes undetected (dwell time)
audit-cm01.explanation: Continuous monitoring shortens detection time dramatically.

audit-cm02.text: Why do signature-based SIEMs miss living-off-the-land attacks?
options: LOTL activity stays below individual rule thresholds and uses legitimate tools
audit-cm02.explanation: LOTL blends into normal admin activity, evading fixed signatures.

audit-cm02.text: What's the purpose of schema normalization (OCSF/ECS) at ingest?
options: A single detection rule can match across dozens of data sources
audit-cm02.explanation: Normalized schemas let one rule work across many log types.

audit-cm02.text: Was SolarWinds SUNBURST caught by a SIEM signature on known patterns?
options: No — it was novel and evaded signature detection
audit-cm02.explanation: Novel attacks slip past signatures — behavioral detection is needed.

audit-cm02.text: Which ML technique best detects novel login anomalies with no labeled attack data?
audit-cm02.explanation: Unsupervised outlier detection finds novel anomalies without labels.

audit-cm02.text: What's the primary operational challenge in next-gen SIEM?
audit-cm02.explanation: False-positive reduction is the core day-to-day SIEM challenge.

audit-cm02.text: Supervised ML (e.g. XGBoost) is appropriate when…
options: You have labeled attack data to train on
You have no labels at all
You never want to detect anything
audit-cm02.explanation: Supervised needs labels; unsupervised handles the novel/unlabeled case.

audit-cm02.text: A next-gen SIEM improves on the old model by adding…
options: Behavioral/ML detection on normalized data, not just static signatures
audit-cm02.explanation: ML on normalized telemetry catches what signatures can't.

audit-cm02.text: To catch attacks that stay below rule thresholds, you need…
options: Anomaly detection that models normal and flags deviations
audit-cm02.explanation: Anomaly detection surfaces stealthy, sub-threshold behavior.

audit-cm03.text: How does UEBA catch an attacker using valid stolen credentials?
options: By comparing behavior against that account's own baseline
audit-cm03.explanation: Valid creds pass auth, but anomalous behavior versus baseline reveals misuse.

audit-cm03.text: What's the advantage of UEBA risk-score chaining over single-rule alerts?
options: Multiple low-confidence signals compound into a high-confidence alert, cutting false positives
It removes the need for baselines
audit-cm03.explanation: Chaining weak signals reduces noise while raising confidence.

audit-cm03.text: Is UEBA reliable from its first day of deployment?
options: No — it needs a baseline period to learn normal behavior
audit-cm03.explanation: UEBA must learn baselines before detection is accurate.

audit-cm03.text: Which signal would have flagged the Tesla insider earliest?
options: Large HR file downloads and USB activity outside normal patterns
audit-cm03.explanation: Behavioral anomalies (bulk downloads, USB) are the insider tell.

audit-cm03.text: Should a CFO and a junior analyst share one UEBA baseline?
options: No — each role/user has its own behavioral baseline
audit-cm03.explanation: Normal differs by role and user; baselines must be per-entity.

options: Modeling normal behavior and flagging deviations
audit-cm03.explanation: It's a behavioral lens, not a signature engine.

options: Insider threats and credential misuse that look like 'authorized' activity
audit-cm03.explanation: It catches misuse by accounts that are technically authorized.

options: Reducing false positives so analysts trust the high-confidence alerts
audit-cm03.explanation: Compounding signals raises precision and reduces fatigue.

audit-cm04.text: Can NDR detect C2 beaconing in encrypted HTTPS without decrypting it?
options: Yes — via connection timing and payload-size patterns
audit-cm04.explanation: Beaconing has rhythmic timing/size patterns visible without decryption.

audit-cm04.text: Why is NDR uniquely valuable for IoT/OT devices?
options: It passively observes network traffic with no agent on the device
audit-cm04.explanation: Agentless observation covers devices that can't run EDR agents.

audit-cm04.text: What anomaly did NDR-equipped orgs catch in the Hafnium Exchange attack?
options: Exchange servers initiating unexpected outbound external connections
audit-cm04.explanation: Servers deviating from their expected network role is a behavioral tell.

audit-cm04.text: What can JA3 TLS fingerprints do even on encrypted traffic?
options: Identify specific malware families by their TLS handshake signature
audit-cm04.explanation: JA3 identifies C2/malware by handshake characteristics without decryption.

audit-cm04.text: A web server making large unexpected outbound HTTPS connections is…
options: Abnormal — a deviation from its expected role
audit-cm04.explanation: NDR baselines each device's role and flags deviations.

options: Covering unmanaged/agentless devices EDR can't reach
audit-cm04.explanation: NDR sees the network; EDR sees the endpoint — together they cover more.

options: Analyzing traffic behavior (timing, volume, peers) rather than payloads
audit-cm04.explanation: Network behavior reveals C2 and lateral movement without decryption.

options: Malicious traffic is encrypted, so behavioral analysis beats payload inspection
audit-cm04.explanation: With pervasive encryption, behavior is the visible signal.

audit-cm05.text: Does CSPM require an agent on every cloud resource?
options: No — it audits configuration via the cloud provider's APIs
audit-cm05.explanation: CSPM is agentless, reading config through provider APIs.

audit-cm05.text: What does CSPM attack-path analysis model that single-resource checks can't?
options: How chained misconfigurations let an attacker reach sensitive data
Whether one resource meets a CIS control
audit-cm05.explanation: Graph-based analysis finds multi-step paths to sensitive data.

audit-cm05.text: Which would CSPM flag as the most severe?
options: A public EC2 with an IAM role granting Action:* Resource:* to all S3
An S3 bucket missing server-side encryption
audit-cm05.explanation: Internet-facing + full S3 access is a complete data-exposure path.

audit-cm05.text: Was the Capital One breach caused by a software vulnerability or a misconfiguration?
options: A software vulnerability in a cloud service
audit-cm05.explanation: It was a misconfiguration chain — exactly what CSPM attack-path analysis catches.

options: From per-resource config checks to graph-based attack-path analysis
From graphs back to manual review
audit-cm05.explanation: Modern CSPM reasons about chains, not just isolated resources.

audit-cm05.text: Why is attack-path context more useful than a flat findings list?
options: It prioritizes the misconfigs that actually combine to expose data
audit-cm05.explanation: Path context shows which findings truly matter together.

options: Continuous monitor of cloud config and attack paths
audit-cm05.explanation: Continuous CSPM catches misconfig and new paths as they appear.

audit-cm05.text: To reduce CSPM-identified risk, you should…
options: Fix the misconfigurations that form the path to sensitive data first
audit-cm05.explanation: Breaking the chain to sensitive data is the highest-leverage fix.

audit-cm06.text: What's the relationship between STIX and TAXII?
options: STIX is the data model for threat intel; TAXII is the transport for sharing it
TAXII is the model; STIX the transport
audit-cm06.explanation: STIX structures the intel; TAXII moves it between parties.

audit-cm06.text: What does a Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) do?
options: Aggregates, deduplicates, and scores feeds, then exposes a unified TAXII endpoint
Generates new malware signatures in a sandbox
audit-cm06.explanation: A TIP consolidates and scores intel before it reaches the SIEM.

audit-cm06.text: Was Volt Typhoon detected mainly by signature rules matching malware hashes?
options: No — it was living-off-the-land, evading hash/signature detection
audit-cm06.explanation: LOTL actors like Volt Typhoon evade signatures; behavior is the signal.

audit-cm06.text: Why do STIX indicators carry confidence scores and expiry dates?
options: To stop stale or low-confidence IoCs from generating false positives
audit-cm06.explanation: Scoring and expiry keep old/weak indicators from creating noise.

audit-cm06.text: What does CISA's Automated Indicator Sharing (AIS) provide?
options: Free STIX/TAXII threat-intel access to subscribing organizations
audit-cm06.explanation: AIS offers free machine-speed indicator sharing via STIX/TAXII.

audit-cm06.text: The point of a threat-intel pipeline is to…
options: Turn many raw feeds into scored, deduplicated indicators the SIEM can act on
Collect feeds and never use them
audit-cm06.explanation: Aggregation + scoring makes intel actionable instead of overwhelming.

audit-cm06.text: Adding more raw feeds without a TIP tends to…
options: Increase false positives from stale, duplicate, low-confidence IoCs
audit-cm06.explanation: Unmanaged feeds create noise; scoring/dedup is what helps.

audit-cm06.text: Against LOTL actors, intel should be paired with…
options: Behavioral detection, since hashes/signatures alone won't catch them
audit-cm06.explanation: LOTL needs behavior-based detection alongside curated intel.

options: Incident response actions via playbooks (orchestration + automated response)
audit-cm07.explanation: SOAR orchestrates and automates response steps.

audit-cm07.text: Should a SOAR playbook auto-disable a CEO's account with no approval?
options: No — high-impact actions need a human approval step

audit-cm07.text: What's the primary metric for SOAR's value?
audit-cm07.explanation: SOAR shortens response time, so MTTR is the key metric.

options: Enrich context first, then respond once the decision is confirmed
audit-cm07.explanation: Enriching before acting prevents wrong automated responses.

audit-cm07.text: Which SOAR action could have limited Twilio's cascading 2022 impact?
options: Auto-suspending affected accounts within minutes of anomalous authentication
Blocking all SMS until manual review
audit-cm07.explanation: Fast automated containment on anomalous auth limits cascade.

audit-cm07.text: SOAR playbooks should be treated as…
options: Production code — version controlled, peer reviewed, staged rollout
audit-cm07.explanation: Playbooks act on prod systems, so they need software rigor.

options: Consistent, fast response that scales beyond manual analyst speed
audit-cm07.explanation: Automation cuts MTTR while keeping humans for high-impact calls.

options: Automate enrichment and low-risk containment; gate high-impact actions on approval
audit-cm07.explanation: Automate the safe steps; keep humans on the consequential ones.

audit-cm08.text: Why do honeytokens generate so few false positives?
options: Legitimate users have no reason to touch them — any access is suspicious
audit-cm08.explanation: A honeytoken touch is almost always malicious — very high signal.

audit-cm08.text: Why is an AD honeypot account with a Kerberoastable SPN effective?
options: A ticket request for the decoy SPN reveals an attacker doing privilege-esc recon
audit-cm08.explanation: Requesting the decoy's ticket exposes the attacker's reconnaissance.

audit-cm08.text: Does an AWS honeytoken key with a deny-all policy create operational risk if used?
options: No — deny-all means it grants no access; the use just triggers an alert
Yes — the attacker gains AWS access
audit-cm08.explanation: A deny-all honeytoken is safe: it only signals, never grants access.

audit-cm08.text: What deception would have warned during the 2022 Uber lateral movement?
options: Honeytokens — fake credentials placed alongside real ones in internal repos
audit-cm08.explanation: Honeytokens in repos catch attackers harvesting credentials.

options: It raises the probability a laterally-moving attacker trips one
audit-cm08.explanation: Dense decoys make it hard for an attacker to avoid them all.

options: Planting traps that only an intruder would interact with
audit-cm08.explanation: Decoys turn attacker curiosity into a high-confidence alert.

audit-cm08.text: Deception is especially good at catching…
options: Attackers during lateral movement and credential harvesting
audit-cm08.explanation: Post-foothold movement is exactly when decoys get tripped.

options: They're cheap to deploy yet produce near-zero-false-positive alerts
audit-cm08.explanation: Minimal cost, maximal signal — a strong detective control.

audit-cm09.text: Does Zero Trust grant implicit trust once you're inside the network?
options: No — every request is authenticated and authorized regardless of location
audit-cm09.explanation: Zero Trust replaces 'trust but verify' with 'never trust, always verify'.

audit-cm09.text: What triggered Google to build BeyondCorp?
options: Operation Aurora — an attack that exploited implicit internal-network trust
A NIST mandate to drop VPNs
audit-cm09.explanation: Aurora exposed the danger of trusting internal traffic by default.

audit-cm09.text: Under Zero Trust CARTA, is a session's trust fixed at initial authentication?
options: No — trust is continuously re-evaluated and can change mid-session
audit-cm09.explanation: CARTA continuously assesses risk and adapts trust during the session.

audit-cm09.text: Which Zero Trust component makes the allow/deny decision?
audit-cm09.explanation: The PDP evaluates policy and decides; the PEP enforces it.

audit-cm09.text: Why does Zero Trust produce richer audit telemetry?
options: Every access decision — not just security events — is logged
audit-cm09.explanation: Per-request decisions create a far more complete audit trail.

options: Never trust, always verify — location grants no trust
audit-cm09.explanation: Verification is per-request, independent of network position.

audit-cm09.text: Zero Trust most directly defends against…
options: Lateral movement after an initial compromise
audit-cm09.explanation: Re-verifying every request blocks the free movement breaches rely on.

audit-cm09.text: Auditing a Zero Trust deployment leans on…
options: The per-decision telemetry to verify policy is enforced everywhere
audit-cm09.explanation: Decision logs let auditors confirm Zero Trust is actually enforced.

audit-cm10.text: How does XDR differ from juggling separate EDR/NDR/identity consoles?
options: It auto-correlates signals across sources into one incident view
audit-cm10.explanation: XDR unifies and correlates telemetry rather than forcing manual pivoting.

audit-cm10.text: How does XDR entity-graph correlation link alerts from different sources?
options: By finding alerts sharing the same entity (host, IP, user) within a time window
By matching alert text to a description library
audit-cm10.explanation: Shared entities within a window stitch alerts into one incident.

audit-cm10.text: Why does XDR tag alerts with MITRE ATT&CK techniques?
options: To show the attack-chain narrative instead of isolated alerts
audit-cm10.explanation: ATT&CK tagging reveals the story across the kill chain.

audit-cm10.text: What detection failure was XDR designed to address (seen in the Lapsus$ Microsoft breach)?
options: Disparate signals across sources weren't correlated into one incident view in time
audit-cm10.explanation: Uncorrelated signals across tools delayed recognizing the incident.

audit-cm10.text: XDR's core value is that analysts work…
options: Grouped incidents, not thousands of isolated alerts
A single attack shows up as weak signals in multiple tools that only mean something together
audit-cm10.explanation: The full picture emerges only when signals are joined.

options: Cutting the time to understand and respond to an incident
audit-cm10.explanation: One incident view speeds investigation and response.

audit-cm10.text: XDR helps prevent the 'missed-in-the-noise' breach by…
options: Stitching endpoint, network, and identity signals into a single timeline
audit-cm10.explanation: A unified timeline surfaces what siloed tools miss.

audit-cm11.text: Do annual compliance audits give a continuous real-time view?
options: No — they're point-in-time; continuous compliance is the real-time view
audit-cm11.explanation: Annual audits are snapshots; continuous compliance monitors constantly.

audit-cm11.text: How do continuous compliance platforms collect control evidence?
options: Automated, scheduled API calls to cloud and IdP systems
audit-cm11.explanation: API-driven, scheduled collection replaces manual gathering.

audit-cm11.text: Roughly how much can continuous compliance cut audit prep time?
audit-cm11.explanation: Automated evidence drastically reduces audit crunch.

audit-cm11.text: Why was the FTC Drizly consent order significant?
options: It established that individual executives can be personally liable for systemic compliance failures
It required a one-time audit only
audit-cm11.explanation: Drizly extended accountability to executives personally.

audit-cm11.text: In continuous compliance, when is control drift detected?
options: Immediately — not at the next annual audit
audit-cm11.explanation: Continuous monitoring flags drift the moment it happens.

audit-cm11.text: The value of continuous compliance is…
options: Ongoing verification that controls stay effective between audits
audit-cm11.explanation: It proves controls hold continuously, not just on audit day.

options: Specific framework controls (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI) it satisfies
audit-cm11.explanation: Each evidence item proves a specific control.

audit-cm11.text: Cases like Drizly mean compliance programs increasingly need…
options: Demonstrable, continuous control effectiveness — not point-in-time box-checking
audit-cm11.explanation: Personal-liability precedents raise the bar for ongoing proof.

audit-cm12.text: What is a SOC maturity scorecard for?
options: Assessing and improving the SOC's detection/response capability over time
audit-cm12.explanation: Maturity scoring drives targeted improvement of the SOC.

audit-cm12.text: What's the minimum recommended SOC maturity target (CREST model)?
options: Level 3 (Proactive — threat hunting and behavioral detection)
audit-cm12.explanation: Proactive (L3) is the recommended enterprise minimum.

audit-cm12.text: Is a false-positive rate above 5% acceptable for a mature SOC?
options: No — high FP rates cause alert fatigue and missed real threats
audit-cm12.explanation: Low false-positive rates are a hallmark of maturity.

audit-cm12.text: Did the MGM 2023 breach show that faster tools alone would have fully prevented the $100M loss?
options: No — it's not purely a technical/MTTD problem; process and response maturity matter too
audit-cm12.explanation: Detection speed helps, but process/response maturity is equally vital.

audit-cm12.text: What SOAR automation coverage is needed for sub-hour MTTR at scale?
options: Greater than 70% of alert types covered by automated playbooks
audit-cm12.explanation: High automation coverage is what enables fast response at scale.

audit-cm12.text: Which pair of metrics best gauges SOC effectiveness?
options: MTTD (time to detect) and MTTR (time to respond)
audit-cm12.explanation: Detection and response times are the core SOC performance metrics.

audit-cm12.text: Moving from reactive to mature means…
options: Proactive threat hunting and behavioral detection, low FP rates, high automation
audit-cm12.explanation: Maturity is proactive, low-noise, and highly automated.

options: Mature security is continuous, automated, and measured — not periodic and manual
audit-cm12.explanation: Continuous, automated, measured monitoring is the modern standard.
